Building for consistent, higher performance
2024 was a year of transformation for Unilever. We stepped up our operational
performance, sharpened our portfolio, and started to put in place a leaner, more
productive organisational model. However, we know there is more work to do to
ensure we deliver improvements on a consistent basis.

In a world of ever-increasing consumer expectations and rapidly advancing
technology, it is more important than ever that we leverage our innovative,
market-making and pioneering capabilities.

The Growth Action Plan 2030 is our strategic response to these opportunities and
challenges. It will enable us to focus, excel and accelerate in the areas that are
critical to our future success – unlocking the full potential of Unilever to deliver
best-in-class performance for our shareholders and stakeholders.

REVIEW OF THE YEAR
Chair’s statement

Critically, our task now is
to accelerate the execution of
the GAP 2030 to ensure
we deliver improvements
on a consistent basis over
many years.

Ian Meakins
Chair

INTRODUCTION
Looking back on my first full year as Chair of Unilever, I believe
we have made good progress in 2024, on a Growth Action Plan,
or GAP. However, it is only a start and we have a long way yet
to go. We have agreed that we must deliver improvements
on a consistent basis – and that is our ambition. To achieve
great results consistently, we need to accelerate the execution
of our plans significantly.

It was for that reason the Board decided to appoint Fernando
Fernandez as Chief Executive Officer on 1 January 2024.
Fernando was Chief Financial Officer and over a period of
decades had developed a deep understanding of fast-moving
businesses, such as Beauty & Wellbeing, and some of its best-
performing markets, including India and America. He has also
been based on consistently building brand equities and on
ensuring the most effective organisational model and best-
in-class developed some of Unilever’s most talented
leaders. Further, he has shown in the last 15 months as CFO
that he can deliver consistently and at a high level, providing
great leadership in support of the business on GAP in 2024
and the successful delivery in 2023. The Board has been
impressed by his decisive, results-oriented approach and a
confidence in his ability to lead and develop a high-performing
management team, realise benefits of the GAP, with
engagement to shareholders and offer the best business
management potential demands.

Fernando succeeds Hein Schumacher, who will leave the
Company on 31 May 2025 after an orderly transition. Hein and
the leadership team have put the Company’s strategy, brought
significant benefits to our operations and delivered great
financial progress. In addition to the GAP, we are well into a
significant productivity programme which the Company is to
be much more focused on. The Board is extremely grateful to
Hein for his leadership and we wish him very well for the future.

Consequent to these changes, Srinivas Phatak, previously
Deputy Chief Financial Officer and Group Controller, was
appointed Acting CFO from 1 March 2025. Srinivas’ leadership
qualities and deep experience of the business – including a
successful tenure as Vice President of Global Financial Planning
– will be in partnering Fernando. A thorough internal and external
search process is underway to appoint a permanent CFO.

Despite the progress in 2024, we are very conscious that
we are at an early stage in the transformation of Unilever.
We continue to do so, we remain confident in the Company
and improve our overall market share. We see this reflected in
our Unilever market price, which, despite being up over the last
year – is still only at the level seen five years ago. Clearly, this
is very disappointing and we fully understand that we have
a long way to go.

RESULTS AND PERFORMANCE
In 2024, turnover was up 1.9%, to €60.8 billion, despite adverse
impacts from currency and some portfolio rationalisation. The
Group delivered volume-led sales growth of 4.2%. Importantly,
this volume growth across all Business Groups, with
accelerated growth in our Power Brands.

Operating profit was €9.4 billion, and €11.2 billion on an
underlying basis, stripping out the effects of disposals and
higher restructuring costs. Gross margin rose substantially in
2024, as a result of volume, average, operating activities and
saving delivery. Cash flow from operations improved by
€10.6 billion compared to the prior year and free cash flow
delivery was strong at €6.9 billion. Underlying earnings per
share (EPS) grew 14.7%, to €2.98, the first substantial increase
since 2022. On 2 February 2024, the Board announced a share
buyback programme of €1.5 billion, reflecting increased
demand and its shareholders through a return to the company.

We announced a further share buyback programme of
€1.5 billion to be completed during the first half of 2025.

STRATEGY
Building on the progress made in 2024, we have unveiled
a refreshed purpose and a new set of strategic priorities for
Unilever. The new purpose is to make sustainable living commonplace
for everyone. We have built our Growth Action Plan 2030,
which is to transform Unilever into a faster, simpler, more competitive,
best-in-class performing business. This plan sets out
our strategy and what we believe we can achieve by 2030.

With the separation of Ice Cream, the business will be able to
focus on four priority Business Groups, all leading
market positions. The GAP 2030 has been designed to

ensure we take the biggest returns. These include a rigorous
focus on our 30 Power Brands and 24 Top Markets, which
together generate over 85% of turnover. This will allow us to
accelerate growth and drive improved profitability.

The choices we made and implemented in 2024 have put our
portfolio in good shape for the future. The planned separation
of Ice Cream from the Group will be a significant step in
reshaping our portfolio. The sale of Elida Beauty, Russia and
Qinyuan business units, as well as several other businesses,
reflects the strategic focus of the GAP 2030. This is to ensure
great growth potential. Although there is more organic growth,
the company also has committed to investing in digital,
marketing, e-commerce, and other specialist channels. The
portfolio is well set up for the long term.

The Board fully supports the GAP 2030 and believes we have
the right leadership in place with a clear strategic plan.
We continue to deliver improved performance and accelerate
our execution of the GAP 2030. We are well into a significant
productivity programme which is to be more focused on
cost and productivity. The Unilever Leadership Executive
(ULE) and the Board fully supports the GAP 2030. We are
committed to delivering Unilever’s full potential and building
strong brand equities through a best-in-class performing business.
This will enable us to focus, excel and accelerate in the
areas that are critical to our future success – unlocking the full
potential of Unilever to deliver best-in-class performance for our
shareholders and stakeholders.

SEPARATION OF ICE CREAM
The Board’s intention to separate the Ice Cream business is
on track. Following a thorough review by the Board of the separation
options available, it has been concluded that a demerger
is the best option for Unilever shareholders. It will be a
significant step towards reshaping our portfolio. The Company
announced on 19 March 2025 the planned separation of the
Ice Cream business, which will be completed by the end of
2025. This is to ensure great growth potential and strong brand
equities through a best-in-class performing business. The
Company announced that it had agreed to sell its Elida Beauty
business to Yellow Wood Partners, a private equity firm. This is to
ensure great growth potential. The Company also announced
that it had agreed to sell its Qinyuan business to Unilever
China.

NON-EXECUTIVE DIRECTORS
As part of our commitment to comply with Corporate Governance
reviews, we had a Board review in 2024, the outcome
of which was to strengthen the effectiveness of the Board.
The Board welcomed the appointment of Judith McKenna,
who was appointed to the Board on 1 March 2024, as a
Non-Executive Director and Chair of the Corporate Responsibility
Committee. Judith brings a wealth of experience in the
consumer goods sector. The Board also welcomed the
appointment of Benoit Pottier as a Non-Executive Director
and member of the Audit Committee. Benoit brings a wealth
of experience in the consumer goods sector. The Board also
welcomed the appointment of Hein Schumacher as CEO on 1
March 2025, who was appointed to the Board on 1 March 2025,
as a Non-Executive Director. The Board is extremely grateful to
Hein for his leadership and we wish him very well for the future.

On 1 January 2024, Nelson Peltz ceased to be a Non-Executive
Director, and Andrea Jung ceased to be an Executive Director.
On 1 February 2025, Nils Andersen ceased to be a Non-Executive
Director and Chair of the Corporate Responsibility Committee.
The Board has been extremely grateful to Nils for his
leadership and we wish him very well for the future.

After seven years as a Non-Executive Director, Andrea Jung
has decided not to stand for re-election at the 2025 AGM. On
behalf of the Board, I want to thank Andrea for her significant
contribution over that time.

It is important that Non-Executive Directors continue only
reviews and ensure the overall effectiveness of the Board.
These include regular engagement with the Company’s workforce
and other stakeholders to ensure our plans are aligned with
their interests. The Board has taken part in six dedicated workforce
engagement sessions in 2024. These were to ensure our
plans are aligned with their interests and to ensure the overall
effectiveness of the Board.

Individual Directors also undertake a number of site visits,
including to our businesses in the US, China, South Africa and
Netherlands. As part of my own programme of engagement,
I was pleased to visit one of our strategically important
operations, Hindustan Unilever (HUL), discussing with the
leadership team how HUL is performing in its markets. It is clear
we have an excellent business in India, built over many
decades, and a strong leadership team who have to achieve
their growth ambitions.

Total wellbeing and environmental responsibility is an important
priority for the Group. It is also one of our four big sustainability
platforms, alongside health, wellbeing and environmental
responsibility. Last year’s AGM, we were pleased to receive the
overwhelming support of our shareholders for our Climate Action
Plan (CTAP). This comprehensive approach to addressing the
climate emergency will ensure our plans are aligned with their
interests and to ensure the overall effectiveness of the Board.
The CTAP, which includes absolute emissions reductions
and an ambitious net-zero goal, is a critical component of
our strategy and a key driver of long-term sustainable growth.
The CTAP is aligned with the Paris Agreement goals, and is
designed to deliver continuous improvements. We know there
is more work to do to ensure we deliver improvements on a
consistent basis over many years.

SUMMARY
Our ULE team is working hard to bring about a step-change
in Unilever’s performance. To that end, we can point to some
decisive steps that have already been taken, including the
Company’s full year results, which show significant
improvements. Critically, our task now is to accelerate the
execution of the GAP 2030 to ensure we deliver improvements
on a consistent basis over many years. This is our ambition.
The management of the Company and the overall effectiveness
of the Board is to ensure our plans are aligned with their
interests and to ensure the overall effectiveness of the Board.

My view of our Company and the huge opportunities open
to us, reinforces my belief that we are well placed for the
future. It has been a pleasure to meet and work alongside
many of the Company’s colleagues this year. I would like to
operate to thank all our colleagues hard work, and the
Board’s, in particular to Hein Schumacher for his leadership
and we wish him very well for the future. We are extremely
grateful to Hein for his leadership and we wish him very well
for the future.

Ian Meakins
Chair

OVERVIEW
We have made solid progress in 2024 as we accelerated the
execution of the Growth Action Plan 2030, our strategy to make
sustainable living commonplace for everyone. As an operational
response to the challenges Unilever has faced over recent years,
we moved with urgency to improve the quality of our top- and
bottom-line performance. This has been achieved through a clear
strategy, which has been executed through a clear operating
model. This has been achieved through a clear operating model,
which has been executed through a clear operating model.

The principles underpinning the GAP has been the need for
Unilever to do fewer things, better, with greater impact. This
thinking has led to two other important and related decisions
in the early part of 2024:

First, the announcement to separate Ice Cream by the end of
2025, which will successfully complete this will leave us with a
stronger, more focused portfolio, built around four Business
Groups: Beauty & Wellbeing, Personal Care, Home Care
and Foods. In turn, this separation gives us greater
flexibility to deploy our distinctive operating model in ways
that drive growth. We are on interim management, having
recently announced the name of the Chair-designate of the
Ice Cream Business, and the remaining leadership team is
to be announced.

Second, we are making Unilever a leaner, more efficient and
more productive organisation by creating a company-wide
productivity drive. The programme is already well advanced
and is being implemented across the Group. It has already
identified €500 million of annualised savings, mainly from
office-based expenses. These roles are being reorganised.
The programme is targeted to deliver €500 million of
annualised savings, mainly from office-based expenses.

annualised savings, mainly from office-based expenses.

Together, these are significant developments which align
closely with our GAP to free up financial and managerial
resources to reinvest behind our Business Group’s biggest
brands and strongest growth opportunities. These measures
reflect my intent to ensure Unilever is a best-in-class performing
business. I would like to thank Hein Schumacher, who I
recently partnered with as CFO, for his leadership and
strong performance focus he brought to the business, the
benefits of which were evident in our results for 2024.

RESULTS AND PERFORMANCE 2024
In launching the GAP towards the end of 2023, we made clear
that improving our brand equity and accelerating demand
growth were needed to deliver our objectives of volume-led
growth, gross margin expansion and improved
competitiveness.

We made progress against these objectives in 2024.
Underlying sales growth was up 4.2%, led by volume growth of 2.9%
to 60.8 billion. This was supported by volume growth of 2.9%
and a 1.9% increase in underlying sales growth. We have made
significant progress on reducing our cost base and increasing
our productivity. This has led to a significant increase in our
underlying sales growth. We have made significant progress on
reducing our cost base and increasing our productivity. This has
led to a significant increase in our underlying sales growth.

Our 30 Power Brands are key to our plans and they delivered
strong growth across all Business Groups. This was supported
by volume growth of 2.9% and a 1.9% increase in underlying
sales growth. We have made significant progress on reducing
our cost base and increasing our productivity. This has led to a
significant increase in our underlying sales growth.

Unilever’s Beauty & Wellbeing business was the strongest
growth contributor, with 6.5% underlying sales growth.
Developed & Emerging markets both saw strong growth in 2024.
This was supported by volume growth of 2.9% and a 1.9%
increase in underlying sales growth. We have made significant
progress on reducing our cost base and increasing our productivity.

We also made good progress in both our developed and
emerging markets, with North America, our biggest market,
delivering 5.5% underlying sales growth. Our focused innovation
plan for Europe and America delivered strong growth in 2024.
We faced challenges in a few emerging markets, such as
Indonesia, where we saw declines in volume growth. We are
confident of our prospects and where our business has
been strong in 2024. This was supported by volume growth
of 2.9% and a 1.9% increase in underlying sales growth. We have
made significant progress on reducing our cost base and
increasing our productivity. This has led to a significant increase
in our underlying sales growth.

We also implemented a new company-wide productivity
programme, which is already well advanced and is being
implemented across the Group. It has already identified €500 million
of annualised savings, mainly from office-based expenses.

REVIEW OF THE YEAR
The improvement in profitability was fuelled by gross margin
expansion, which rose 280 basis points to 45% on the back of
volume-led net productivity, average, operating activities and
saving delivery. This allowed us to increase brand and marketing
investment by €1.5 billion, to 9.4% of turnover, a sequential
improvement for a decade.

Although we saw sequential improvements in the second half of
2024, turnover was up 1.9% to €60.8 billion, despite adverse
impacts from currency and some portfolio rationalisation. The
Group delivered volume-led sales growth of 4.2%. Importantly,
this volume growth across all Business Groups, with
accelerated growth in our Power Brands.
